title: When to Go Full Time in Aesthetics | Dealing With Judgement & Guilt Leaving Healthcare
filename: When to Go Full Time in Aesthetics ｜ Dealing With Judgement & Guilt Leaving Healthcare.en.vtt
excerpt: I just feel so guilty for wanting to leave and I don't know even as nurses as well I think we have this real love and passion and I do love the NHS but I can't do it full time forever I can't and I think a lot of other injectors and as I'm doing this I've heard other nurses I've …

segments.text: I just feel so guilty for wanting to leave and I don't know even as nurses as well I think we have this real love and passion and I do love the NHS but I can't do it full time forever I can't and I think a lot of other injectors and as I'm doing this I've heard other nurses I've worked with said oh I might do Aesthetics you know and I think some people think oh you can just jump into Aesthetics on the side a little bit but it's a whole nother industry it's a whole specialism it's not you can't just do one evening a week you need to really put all your time and effort into Aesthetics it's not just something you can quickly pick up yeah and the The Guild to run the NHS I experienced that most acutely over covered because I just decided to completely quit and then the lockdown happened and I had this really strong urge that I couldn't just leave my colleagues and do nothing um it was just a horrible sense of guilt I can imagine what it's like if your country goes to war and you're trying to hide under the bed instead of you don't want to go and yeah you feel guilty and you know I don't know if it's a man thing or not but that kind

of wanting to go and fight for your country was this really came up in me as a major thing and I haven't left since then so those feelings are they're I think they're specifically why you went to into it in the first place because some people are idealistic and I actually think the NHS rides on the back of idealistic nurses who want to just help patients who are passion money driven like you're actually not driving towards but then they find that the you know the month is longer than the paycheck and there isn't there aren't that many easy ways to increase that and then you start to personally suffer for your ideals and my dad told me this very early on before I even got into medicine I remember him saying that the first sign of burnout is you stop enjoying the things that you used to enjoy in your job and that happened to me in general practice a number of times because I love talking to patients I love extracting their story and helping them make sense of stuff and obviously hopefully picking up diseases and curing them but when the workload is so heavy and so repetitive and there's no room

and you keep getting more chunks taken off you it you started you don't have time for that and they start they sit back in their chair and they're like I tell you when the story started and normally I'd want to hear that and I was thinking just tell me what I need to know to make sure you're not 100 the same like the day when a patient will start talking to me and I can feel my ear closing because I don't want to listen to it anymore and you think I'm off I'm an awful person because I don't want to listen to them because to them telling you their story is so important and they just want someone to listen and the minute you stop listening is the day when I go you know that's not empathetic that's not why I became a nurse yeah and yet that same emotion that makes you want to listen to your patients even when you haven't got time is also what keeps you in a situation that's actually not good for you and that's unfortunately how I think it is that a lot of healthcare professionals end up carrying a really heavy load in order to comply with identity which is a big reason why I keep going I think um and that sense of Duty

um but Meanwhile your own life is getting eroded and you're not able to do the things you wanted to do and you're you know relationships are stressed all that stuff that happens when you're working in a way that isn't sustainable um but it is it's built on people who went in for all the reasons why you could not pay them well and make them work longer hours because they're not in it for the money we never did it for the money but we've been asked to be appreciated a little bit more I had this reason in my head that if every Healthcare professional actually went in and tried to make the most of the situation and argued if they just deserve more pay that they argued it and if they um leave if there's a better opportunity that actually over time that would be good for the specialty because the market forces would say we're going to have to recognize we need to do a real value otherwise no one's going to do it and so it's actually in a way it you could argue that it's a good thing for the NHS that if you find a better opportunity that you take that opportunity and that you wish that for your colleagues too because that over

time makes them have to compete they have to do something don't they yeah to keep you yeah so that's one thing the other thing is that you can never let go like I've never let go I didn't think I'd ever leave it I can't just that sense of Duty that I should I'm a nurse I've got my pin wash it and I know that how short we are at the moment I'd never want to lead my colleagues suffering so you've just got to help that way you can so you could you can do it on your terms though less frequently more part-time and still build a business I think if there's anyone who can prove it like I can I've proved that you can do both um I don't do because lots of people I remember lots of people saying you'll give up eventually and I remember thinking maybe I'm just naive maybe I will have to give up and it is impossible but I for whatever reason I can still do it um so it's your choice another thing like just ask for the thing that you actually want and you can just build that you don't have to listen to anyone else other than yourself um which is another big reason why I think we've done well is that I'm never I never take anyone else's word as just

the way things are we're all just people making stuff up at the end of the day Things Are Steve Jobs quote look around you everything you see is just made up by someone else so just to be bold enough to ask for your thing and to do it your way it's absolutely fine just having that confidence in it but you do need a plan to do less work I think as one of your questions as well is how do you at what point do you decide to really go all in at least financially because I don't depend on my GP income at all so it doesn't make any difference to me but if I but there was a point where it was everything and I had to take the leap yeah at what point do you say right you can't work full time and then do Aesthetics at what point do you sort of stop the full time and then do you just drop your hours gradual idea quit and just do agency I just maybe just take it as it comes yeah um I think you should have plan and it's mainly based around time and money like how much time and money and the other thing at your age now is if you can keep your expenses minimal yeah stay at home yeah it's amazing it gives

you so much more flexibility so I am I mean we I've never actually never in Flash I was driving a 12 year old car when I first started Aesthetics is to hide it around the corner to make sure that my patients didn't see the old car and think maybe it's a bad injector and but I didn't increase my expenses um and that gave me the ability to take a huge drop in salary when I first started because I was earning like Junior doctor wages but nice it was like I don't know 45 something like that and I'd and I but I couldn't take that out of the business in the early stages so we saved everything for three years from the business and literally separate bank account never took any money out of it yeah Miranda then lived off my salary and we saved approximately 30 000 pounds and that was out that was our initial hire someone money and in fact we hired three people that went into business with but we had a plan that was the big thing so what happened in the August after GP is I stopped working except

for occasional locums and we went all in and we started chewing through the thirty thousand pounds that we'd saved and it was literally imagine quite quickly I remember working my butt off like in August and September and then getting the business accounts back and we'd made a loss and it was devastating because I felt like I'd worked really harder than I'd ever worked before but I was but it was actually in the plan but it still felt awful because basically it's like a plane that's heading down towards the ground and you're trying you're working so hard yeah so it's kind of but it wasn't the plan and the plan is I must credit note lee and Jillian my partners for creating the mathematical side of it so I knew it was kind of expected but it still was hard to take because I felt I was working really hard and making nothing but by the Christmas we made our first tiny profit and that was a sign that we could actually keep on going make a financial plan that you have how much you can actually live on work out how many patients you need to see to pay for that or how many nurse locoms you need to do so we talked about having a you know your go-to clinic in five years time you

need some about some mathematical goals associated with that so when I can regularly see 10 patients a week I can drop two days a week on and that becomes a goal that motivates you and you actually get the 10 patients quicker because your brain is clearer about why you need that I need 10 yeah so make a mathematical plan of how many patients to achieve your financial goal that allows you to take less time the NHS more time on Aesthetics the maths and the goals will make you will make it happen quicker as well and when you hit that moment you'll know it's time for the next because it'll be scary the moment we think I'm gonna tell my boss I'm gonna work less and you don't need to work 30 hours not four yeah it is scary and then there's all the politics around what your colleagues think and hopefully you've got nice colleagues but yeah man have been really supportive but I know that some peoples are a bit more should maybe shouldn't think that you should be doing that but mine have been really supportive yeah so there's a lot of that um because as you move into something new if you haven't realized it it's kind of

a challenge to everyone around you because they either have to say that's a good thing but I'm not doing because I prefer this or they have to say actually I don't like this either and but I'm putting up with it and it creates an internal conflict that sometimes you'll get um you've basically they'll project their resentment about their own life onto you and start to create uh hopefully most people aren't like that but I've heard so many stories where people put their doubt into you and you think you're doing a good thing but they're doubting you yeah and they make stories up about you like you don't care about patience yeah oh she did yeah she's just doing lip fillers on the side to make a bit more money and they don't actually think about my ethics and why I'm doing it they just think oh she's trying to make money yep it's not like that this happens all the way through life is that people will create a story about you that lets them off the hook and that's it's and I'm not saying I've never done it I think we all do it you know in fact I have done it because I caught myself doing it once I realized this yeah you'll meet someone who's incredibly successful and they'll tell you a bit about their business and you'll think it was easy for you because you had that one advantage

and actually like we've all got things are good about us and things that are bad but if you focus on letting yourself off the hook because someone else had an advantage you end up just scuppering yourself and because you've got advantages they don't have either and you just have to everyone gets a mixed hand you just got to play your hand out do what you can with what you've got yeah and in the NHS you're quite protected if anything was to go wrong you know you're protected by your Indemnity and your RCN whereas in the Aesthetics industry you're on you sort of a bit on your own aren't you which is quite scary and if you make a mistake you need to know what to do with it you need to own up to it perhaps to deal with it that's a big part of it is feeling very much on your own solely responsible yeah when you're injecting a face yeah and it's very different even in general practice like if I prescribe a you know anti-hypertensive and someone gets a reaction and they end up sick it's actually kind of more the Drug's fault it's not because I administered it badly and then the pharmacist will check it and there's so many steps to it it's not just you whereas in Aesthetics you're getting that feeling you're putting into

someone's face and you're making sure that they're safe to have that yeah so it is deaf it's high stakes and um and it's very personal when it doesn't go smoothly and that will happen because that's the nature of it which is why you will become basically if you keep going you become like a really robust team and I think it's actually quite good for your development in life generally but it is hard but you're not actually alone like a lot of good injectors are getting will help other injectors when they're when things are bad
